attributes.stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with a three-week history of pain in her right knee. Her symptoms start during her morning run and seem to be worse towards the front of the knee. The pain only settles after a period of rest. It may also be aggravated when she has been sitting for a long time and suddenly stands up. The pain is relieved when she stretches her knee. She has no other joint symptoms. The knee joint looks normal, with a full range of movement. There are no points of tenderness, but the pain can be reproduced when the patient extends the knee against resistance. The rest of the examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Advise complete rest and avoidance of all physical activities
Initiate physical therapy focusing on quadriceps strengthening
attributes.explanation:
Initiate physical therapy focusing on quadriceps strengthening: The patient's symptoms of knee pain after exertion or prolonged sitting, aggravation of pain during tonic contraction of quadriceps (i.e., extending knee against resistance), and normal physical findings are consistent with the diagnosis of patellofemoral pain syndrome (PPS). PPS is primarily managed through conservative measures such as pain relief with NSAIDs and quadriceps strengthening exercises. Physical therapy focusing on quadriceps strengthening is the most appropriate initial management for this patient.

Advise complete rest and avoidance of all physical activities: Complete rest is not recommended for PPS, as strengthening exercises are crucial for managing the condition. The patient should avoid activities that aggravate the pain but can gradually resume exercises as tolerated.

Prescribe a course of corticosteroids: Corticosteroids are not the first-line treatment for PPS, as conservative measures such as NSAIDs and physical therapy are more appropriate. Corticosteroids may be considered in cases of severe inflammation or if the patient does not respond to initial management, but they are not the first choice.

MRI knee: Similar to X-rays, MRI is not typically necessary for PPS, as the diagnosis is primarily clinical. MRI would be indicated if there were concerns for a structural knee injury, such as meniscal or ligamentous damage, or if the patient did not improve with conservative management.

Plain X-ray knee: Imaging is typically not necessary for PPS, as the diagnosis is primarily clinical. X-rays would be indicated if there were concerns for a structural knee injury or if the patient did not improve with conservative management.
learningPoints: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is the most common cause of non-traumatic knee pain in young, active adults.
Physical therapy focusing on quadriceps strengthening is the most appropriate initial management for this condition
references: Petersen W, Ellermann A, Gösele-Koppenburg A, Best R, Rembitzki IV, Brüggemann GP, Liebau C. Patellofemoral pain syndrome. Knee Surg Sports Traumatol Arthrosc. 2014 Oct;22(10):2264-74. doi: 10.1007/s00167-013-2759-6. Epub 2013 Nov 13. PMID: 24221245; PMCID: PMC4169618. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24221245/
Gaitonde DY, Ericksen A, Robbins RC. Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome. Am Fam Physician. 2019 Jan 15;99(2):88-94. PMID: 30633480. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30633480/
